prompt:
Last month the intake box died with a disk full of orphaned half-uploads, because the old PHP thing wrote temp files first and asked questions later. I'm replacing it with a small Go handler and this time the rules are enforced while the bytes stream in, not after. Module go-uploadgate, package upload, stdlib only. upload_test.go is the acceptance contract (read-only).

API: Handler(dir string, p Policy) http.Handler, with type Policy { MaxPartBytes, MaxTotalBytes int64; AllowedTypes []string }.

Behavior for POST multipart/form-data:
- Stream each file part straight to a file under dir while hashing it — do NOT slurp parts into memory and do NOT use ParseMultipartForm (that's the exact disease we're curing). Walk the parts with a multipart.Reader.
- Plain form fields (no filename) are ignored and cost nothing. File parts count: any single part over MaxPartBytes, or the running file-byte total over MaxTotalBytes -> 413 with JSON {"error":..., "field": "<part name>"}. Exactly at a limit is fine.
- Part Content-Type goes through mime.ParseMediaType (parameters stripped, so "text/plain; charset=utf-8" is "text/plain"); a part without one counts as application/octet-stream. Types outside p.AllowedTypes -> 415 with the field named.
- Client filenames are hostile: whatever they send (looking at you, "../../evil.txt") the stored file must land inside dir. Handler picks the on-disk name; report it as "path".
- ANY failure — over-size, bad type, malformed/truncated multipart (clients hang up mid-upload constantly) — must leave dir exactly as it started: every file written so far gets removed. No orphans ever again.
- Success -> 201 with a JSON array, one entry per file part in order: {"field","filename","media_type","size","sha256","path"} — sha256 is the lowercase hex of the streamed hash, size the exact byte count. Zero-byte files are legal.
- Request-level rejects: non-POST -> 405; Content-Type not multipart/form-data -> 415; missing boundary or garbage multipart -> 400. All error bodies are JSON with "error".

Verify: go test -race -timeout 30s ./... . The tests hand you a t.TempDir() as dir and check it's empty after every failure, so cleanup is not optional.
